seo.title: CSS Animations and Keyframes - @keyframes, animation-name, spinners, bounce, fade | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master CSS @keyframes animations. Learn animation-name, duration, iteration-count, direction, fill-mode, play-state, and build loading spinners, pulse effects, bouncing balls, typing text, and shake animations. 60+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: css animations tutorial, keyframes css, animation-name, animation-duration, loading spinner css, bounce animation, pulse animation, css typing effect, infinite animation, fill-mode forwards

content.what:
What Are CSS Animations?

While transitions animate between two states (default and hover), CSS animations let you define multi-step animations that can run automatically, loop forever, and move through any number of key points. They are more powerful than transitions and perfect for things like loading spinners, pulse effects, and continuous motion.

Animations have two parts:

  1. @keyframes — defines the animation's steps (what happens at 0%, 50%, 100%, etc.)
  2. animation properties — applied to an element to actually play the animation
@keyframes bounce {
  0%   { transform: translateY(0); }
  50%  { transform: translateY(-30px); }
  100% { transform: translateY(0); }
}

.ball {
  animation: bounce 1s infinite;
}

That is it. The ball now bounces forever, no JavaScript required.
content.why:
Why Learn CSS Animations?

1. Modern Web Is Alive

Loading spinners, animated logos, pulsing buttons, falling snowflakes, marquee tickers, typing text, shimmer effects — every one of these is a CSS animation. If you want to build impressive, modern websites, you need keyframe animations.

2. They Run on the GPU

Like transitions, CSS animations are handled by the browser's rendering engine. When you animate transform or opacity, the browser uses the GPU to achieve smooth 60fps motion with virtually zero CPU cost. JavaScript animations cannot match this performance for simple visual effects.

3. They Run Automatically

Unlike transitions, animations do not need a trigger like :hover. You can start them immediately on page load, loop them forever, or even pause and play them from JavaScript. This makes them perfect for ambient effects and loading indicators.

4. More Control Than Transitions

Transitions have just two states (before and after). Animations can have as many keyframes as you want, letting you create complex motion paths, multi-color pulses, choreographed sequences, and more. If you can imagine it moving, @keyframes can probably animate it.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Defining Keyframes

The @keyframes rule defines a named sequence of style changes. You can use from/to (2 steps) or percentages (any number of steps):

/* Two-step animation */
@keyframes fadeIn {
  from { opacity: 0; }
  to   { opacity: 1; }
}

/* Multi-step animation */
@keyframes wiggle {
  0%   { transform: rotate(0); }
  25%  { transform: rotate(5deg); }
  50%  { transform: rotate(-5deg); }
  75%  { transform: rotate(5deg); }
  100% { transform: rotate(0); }
}

2. Applying the Animation

Once the keyframes are defined, apply them to an element:

.box {
  animation-name: fadeIn;
  animation-duration: 1s;
  animation-timing-function: ease-out;
  animation-delay: 0.5s;
  animation-iteration-count: 1;
  animation-direction: normal;
  animation-fill-mode: forwards;
  animation-play-state: running;
}

3. The Shorthand (Always Use This)

/* Order: name duration timing-function delay iteration-count direction fill-mode */
.box {
  animation: fadeIn 1s ease-out 0.5s 1 normal forwards;
}

/* Minimal: just name and duration */
.spinner {
  animation: spin 1s linear infinite;
}

4. animation-iteration-count

How many times to play the animation:

animation-iteration-count: 1;        /* play once (default) */
animation-iteration-count: 3;        /* play three times */
animation-iteration-count: infinite; /* loop forever */
animation-iteration-count: 2.5;      /* two and a half times */

5. animation-direction

Controls the direction of playback:

animation-direction: normal;            /* 0% to 100% (default) */
animation-direction: reverse;           /* 100% to 0% */
animation-direction: alternate;         /* 0-100, then 100-0, repeat */
animation-direction: alternate-reverse; /* 100-0, then 0-100, repeat */

alternate is incredibly useful for pulses and back-and-forth motions — you only define half the animation, and alternate plays it forward and backward automatically.

6. animation-fill-mode (The Confusing One)

Controls what the element looks like BEFORE and AFTER the animation runs:

animation-fill-mode: none;      /* default — element returns to original styles */
animation-fill-mode: forwards;  /* element keeps the final keyframe's styles */
animation-fill-mode: backwards; /* element uses the first keyframe's styles during delay */
animation-fill-mode: both;      /* both forwards and backwards */

forwards is the most useful: it makes the element stay in the final state after the animation finishes. Without it, the element snaps back to its original styles when the animation ends.

7. Loading Spinner Example

@keyframes spin {
  from { transform: rotate(0deg); }
  to   { transform: rotate(360deg); }
}

.spinner {
  width: 50px; height: 50px;
  border: 5px solid #e5e7eb;
  border-top-color: #a855f7;
  border-radius: 50%;
  animation: spin 1s linear infinite;
}

8. Multiple Animations on One Element

You can apply multiple animations simultaneously by comma-separating them:

.element {
  animation: fadeIn 1s, bounce 2s infinite 1s;
  /* fadeIn runs once for 1s, then bounce runs forever with 1s delay */
}

9. Performance Tips

  • Animate transform and opacity whenever possible — they are GPU-accelerated
  • Avoid animating width, height, top, left, margin — they trigger layout recalculation
  • Use will-change: transform for heavy animations to hint the browser
  • Set @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) to disable animations for users who get motion sickness

codeExamples.title: Loading Spinner
codeExamples.explanation: Three spinner variations. The first is the classic one-color top-border spinner. The second has three colors on different sides with a more organic cubic-bezier. The third uses two pseudo-elements spinning in opposite directions with different durations — a dual-ring effect. All powered by a single @keyframes spin rule.
codeExamples.output: Three rotating loading spinners with different styles — classic, tri-color, and dual-ring.

codeExamples.title: Bouncing Ball
codeExamples.explanation: A realistic bouncing ball with three synchronized animations: (1) the wrapper bounces up and down, (2) the ball itself squashes at the bottom of the bounce (like a real ball compressing), (3) the shadow pulses smaller and lighter as the ball rises. All three use the same duration and timing function to stay in sync.
codeExamples.output: A golden ball bouncing up and down with a subtle squash at impact and a shadow that pulses with the bounce.

codeExamples.title: Typing Text Effect
codeExamples.code: > Hello, I am Aarav the coder
codeExamples.explanation: Classic terminal typing effect built with two animations. The typing animation grows the width from 0 to 29ch (character-width units), with steps(30, end) creating a stepped effect that looks like characters appearing one at a time. The blink animation toggles the cursor border on and off. Both run simultaneously.
codeExamples.output: A terminal window showing text typing out character by character with a blinking cursor.

codeExamples.title: Pulse and Shake Attention Effects
codeExamples.code:
PULSE
SHAKE
GLOW
codeExamples.explanation: Three attention-grabbing effects. Pulse combines scale with an expanding shadow ring. Shake uses multiple translateX values at different percentages to create rapid side-to-side motion. Glow animates box-shadow size and intensity. All three are infinite loops that demand attention.
codeExamples.output: Three boxes. One pulses with an expanding ring, one shakes side to side, and one has a glowing shadow that brightens and dims.

codeExamples.title: Rainbow Text (Color Cycle)
codeExamples.code: SkyCoders
codeExamples.explanation: The heading has a wide horizontal rainbow gradient clipped to its text using -webkit-background-clip: text. The animation slides the background position across, creating a continuous rainbow cycle through the letters. No color properties are animated — only the background position shifts.
codeExamples.output: Giant text showing a continuous rainbow color cycle flowing through the letters.

codeExamples.title: Slide-In Cards on Page Load
codeExamples.code:
Meet Our Team

Aarav
Priya
Rohan
Ishita
Vivaan
Anika
codeExamples.explanation: Staggered entrance animation. Each card starts invisible and 40px below its final position, then slides up and fades in with animation-fill-mode: forwards (so they STAY visible after). Each card has a different animation-delay using :nth-child selectors, creating a beautiful stagger effect on page load.
codeExamples.output: Six gradient cards that slide up into place one after another when the page loads, creating a wave effect.

codeExamples.title: Progress Bar with Indeterminate Loading
codeExamples.code: Loading your dashboard, Meera...
codeExamples.explanation: Two loading indicators. The progress bar slides a colored segment from left to right continuously — the 'indeterminate' loading pattern. Below it, three colored dots bounce in sequence using animation-delay to create a wave. Both use infinite loops and are pure CSS.
codeExamples.output: A loading screen with a horizontal gradient bar sliding across and three bouncing dots below.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting animation-fill-mode: forwards
commonMistakes.error: When the animation ends, the element reverts to its default CSS, not the final keyframe. If the default was translateX(-100%), the card disappears again.
commonMistakes.explanation: forwards tells the element to keep the final keyframe's styles after the animation ends. This is usually what you want for entrance animations.

commonMistakes.title: Animating Properties That Trigger Layout
commonMistakes.error: Animating left triggers a layout recalculation every frame, which is slow. On complex pages or weaker devices, the animation can stutter.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use transform: translateX() instead of left. Transform runs on the GPU and does not trigger layout or paint work — much smoother.

commonMistakes.title: Not Defining the Keyframes Before Using Them
commonMistakes.error: The animation does nothing because the @keyframes rule is missing. The browser will not warn you — it just silently ignores the animation.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always define the @keyframes rule in your CSS. A typo in the animation name also silently fails, so double-check spelling.

commonMistakes.title: Using Animation for Simple State Changes
commonMistakes.error: Using @keyframes animation for a simple hover color change is overkill and less flexible. It also fights with hovering out.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use transition for simple state changes (hover, focus, class toggle) and reserve @keyframes animation for multi-step or looping animations.

summary: @keyframes animationName { ... } defines the animation steps using from/to or percentages (0%, 25%, 50%, 100%).
Apply an animation to an element with animation: name duration timing delay iteration-count direction fill-mode; (shorthand).
animation-iteration-count: infinite makes the animation loop forever. Useful for spinners, pulses, and ambient effects.
animation-direction: alternate plays the animation forward, then backward, then forward again — perfect for back-and-forth motions like pulses.
animation-fill-mode: forwards keeps the element in its final keyframe state after the animation ends. Without it, the element snaps back to its original styles.
animation-delay lets you stagger multiple elements by applying different delays (often with :nth-child) for a wave or cascade effect.
You can apply multiple animations to one element by comma-separating them: animation: fadeIn 1s, bounce 2s infinite 1s;
For best performance, animate transform and opacity. These are GPU-accelerated and run at 60fps. Avoid animating width, height, top, left, margin.
animation-play-state: paused | running lets JavaScript pause and resume an animation.
Respect accessibility: use @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { * { animation: none !important; } } to disable animations for users with motion sensitivity.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between a CSS transition and a CSS animation?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about how many states each has.
topicSpecific.answer: A transition animates between TWO states (a starting and ending value). An animation uses @keyframes to define MANY states and can run automatically, loop, and play without a trigger.
topicSpecific.explanation: Use transitions for simple state changes (hover, focus). Use animations for multi-step movement, loading effects, or anything that should run on its own.

topicSpecific.question: Which rule defines an animation in CSS?
topicSpecific.explanation: @keyframes animationName { ... } defines the steps of the animation. You then reference it with animation-name: animationName.

topicSpecific.answer: The icon rotates continuously, making a full 360-degree turn every 2 seconds, forever.
topicSpecific.explanation: linear gives a constant speed, 2s is the duration, infinite makes it loop forever. This is the classic loading spinner formula.

topicSpecific.question: How do you make an animation loop forever?
topicSpecific.answer: animation-iteration-count: infinite; or in shorthand: animation: spin 1s infinite;
topicSpecific.explanation: The default iteration count is 1 (plays once). infinite loops forever.

topicSpecific.question: What do from and to mean in @keyframes?
topicSpecific.answer: from is equivalent to 0% (start) and to is equivalent to 100% (end). They are shorthand for two-step animations.
topicSpecific.explanation: For multi-step animations, use percentages: 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, etc.

topicSpecific.question: What does animation-direction: alternate do?
topicSpecific.answer: The animation plays forward (0% to 100%), then backward (100% to 0%), then forward again, alternating on every iteration.
topicSpecific.explanation: Alternate is great for pulse effects and back-and-forth motions. You only define half the animation, and alternate plays it both ways.

topicSpecific.question: What does animation-fill-mode: forwards do?
topicSpecific.answer: After the animation finishes, the element keeps the styles from the final keyframe instead of reverting to its original CSS.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without forwards, an element that slides in and stops at opacity 1 would suddenly snap back to opacity 0. forwards preserves the end state.

topicSpecific.question: What is the order of values in the animation shorthand?
topicSpecific.answer: animation: name duration timing-function delay iteration-count direction fill-mode;
topicSpecific.explanation: Minimum required: name and duration. Everything else is optional. You can omit values in the middle, but order matters.

topicSpecific.answer: The button smoothly grows to 110% of its size at the midpoint and shrinks back to 100% at the end, looping forever.
topicSpecific.explanation: At 0% and 100%, scale is 1. At 50%, scale is 1.1. The browser smoothly interpolates between these, creating a gentle pulse.

topicSpecific.question: How do you apply multiple animations to a single element?
topicSpecific.explanation: Separate each animation with a comma. Each runs independently and can have its own duration, delay, and iteration count.

topicSpecific.question: What does animation-play-state: paused do?
topicSpecific.answer: It freezes the animation at its current frame. Change to running to resume from the same point.
topicSpecific.explanation: Useful for JavaScript-driven controls: you can pause an animation on user interaction (like hovering to stop a carousel) and resume when ready.

topicSpecific.question: Why is animating transform smoother than animating left?
topicSpecific.answer: transform is GPU-accelerated and does not trigger layout recalculation or repaint. left changes the element's position in the layout tree, causing the browser to recalculate positions every frame.
topicSpecific.explanation: Rule: use transform (and opacity) for animations whenever possible. These stay on the GPU for buttery-smooth 60fps.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between animation-fill-mode: forwards and backwards?
topicSpecific.answer: forwards keeps the FINAL keyframe state after the animation ends. backwards applies the FIRST keyframe state during the animation's delay period (before it starts).
topicSpecific.explanation: both combines the two — the first frame is visible during delay AND the last frame is kept after the animation ends.

topicSpecific.question: What does animation: spin 1s 2s infinite mean?
topicSpecific.hint: Identify which time is duration and which is delay.
topicSpecific.answer: The animation is named 'spin', runs for 1 second, starts 2 seconds after the element is rendered, and loops infinitely.
topicSpecific.explanation: The first time value is duration, the second is delay. Iteration count follows.

topicSpecific.question: How do you stagger animations on multiple child elements?
topicSpecific.answer: Use :nth-child(N) selectors with different animation-delay values. Example: .card:nth-child(1) { animation-delay: 0.1s; } .card:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 0.2s; }
topicSpecific.explanation: This creates a wave or cascade effect where each element starts its animation slightly after the previous one.

mixed.question: Which value makes an animation run forever?
mixed.explanation: Example: animation-iteration-count: infinite; or in shorthand: animation: spin 1s infinite;

mixed.question: Can an @keyframes rule have more than two steps?
mixed.answer: Yes. You can define any number of percentage-based steps: 0%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 100%, etc.

mixed.question: When should you use @keyframes instead of a transition?
mixed.answer: Use @keyframes when you need multi-step animations, looping/infinite motion, or animations that run automatically without a trigger (like loading spinners, pulses, or entrance effects on page load).
mixed.explanation: Use transitions for simple 2-state changes (hover, focus, class toggle). Use animations for anything more complex or autonomous.

mixed.question: What happens if you apply animation: bounce 1s but never define @keyframes bounce?
mixed.answer: Nothing happens. The browser silently ignores the animation because the keyframes are not defined. There is no error message.
mixed.explanation: A common bug is typos in the animation name. Double-check that the @keyframes name EXACTLY matches the animation-name value.

mixed.question: What does steps(10, end) do as a timing function?
mixed.answer: The animation advances in 10 discrete jumps instead of smoothly interpolating. The 'end' means the change happens at the end of each step.
mixed.explanation: Used for effects like typewriter text, sprite sheet animations, and clock tick marks where you want distinct steps rather than smooth motion.

mixed.question: What timing function is most natural for a loading spinner?
mixed.explanation: A spinner should rotate at a constant speed. Any easing would make the spinner speed up and slow down, which looks unnatural for a circular loading indicator.

mixed.question: What is the difference between alternate and reverse?
mixed.hint: Alternate loops forward-backward; reverse always plays backward.
mixed.answer: reverse plays the animation backward on every iteration (100% to 0%). alternate plays forward (0-100%) on odd iterations and backward (100-0%) on even iterations.
mixed.explanation: alternate is for back-and-forth motions; reverse is for animations that should always play in reverse.

mixed.question: Why is animation-fill-mode: forwards often essential?
mixed.answer: Without forwards, the element snaps back to its original CSS after the animation ends, which often looks wrong for entrance animations (the element would disappear after sliding in).
mixed.explanation: Any time you animate an element to a final state and want it to STAY there, use forwards.

mixed.question: Can you animate CSS custom properties (variables)?
mixed.hint: Yes, but they need special declaration.
mixed.answer: Yes, but you must register the property with @property first so the browser knows its type. Otherwise the browser treats the variable as a string and cannot interpolate.
mixed.explanation: Example: @property --hue { syntax: '<number>'; inherits: false; initial-value: 0; }. Then you can animate the variable smoothly.

mixed.question: What does animation-iteration-count: 2.5 mean?
mixed.answer: The animation plays two and a half times — two full cycles followed by halfway through a third.
mixed.explanation: Iteration count can be a decimal. Useful for animations that should end at a specific intermediate state.

mixed.question: How do you stop animations for users who have motion sickness?
mixed.answer: Use @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { *, *::before, *::after { animation: none !important; transition: none !important; } }
mixed.explanation: This respects the user's OS-level accessibility preference for reduced motion. Always include this in production websites.

mixed.question: Which property controls how fast an animation plays?
mixed.explanation: Duration sets the length of one iteration of the animation. Specified in seconds (1s) or milliseconds (1000ms).

mixed.question: What does 0%, 100% { opacity: 1; } 50% { opacity: 0; } create?
mixed.answer: A blinking effect — the element is visible at the start and end, and invisible in the middle. Paired with infinite, it creates continuous blinking.
mixed.explanation: Combining 0% and 100% into one rule is a common shortcut when the start and end states are identical.

mixed.question: Why can you not animate background-image with a smooth transition between two images?
mixed.answer: background-image values are discrete — the browser cannot interpolate between two different image URLs. It swaps instantly, not smoothly.
mixed.explanation: Workaround: stack two images on top of each other with absolute positioning and animate their opacity for a cross-fade effect.

mixed.question: What is @keyframes short for?
mixed.answer: Keyframes — key moments (frames) in the animation where you define specific styles. The browser interpolates between them.
mixed.explanation: The term 'keyframe' comes from traditional animation, where an animator draws the important poses and assistants fill in the frames between.

mixed.question: What is animation-delay?
mixed.answer: The time to wait before the animation starts playing after the element is rendered. Default is 0s.
mixed.explanation: Useful for staggering multiple elements: give each a different delay so they animate one after another.

mixed.question: What does animation: spin 1s linear infinite do?
mixed.answer: Runs the 'spin' animation for 1 second with constant speed, looping forever.
mixed.explanation: This is the standard loading spinner formula. linear ensures constant rotation speed; infinite loops forever.

mixed.question: Where should you declare @keyframes rules in your stylesheet?
mixed.answer: Anywhere in the stylesheet, as long as they exist BEFORE the browser tries to apply them. By convention, many developers put them near the bottom or in a dedicated section.
mixed.explanation: Unlike JavaScript, CSS order for @keyframes does not affect whether they work — they are globally scoped within the stylesheet. But grouping them together improves readability.

mixed.answer: The last definition wins. At 50%, background is blue (the later rule overrides the earlier one).
mixed.explanation: Just like regular CSS rules, later declarations override earlier ones when specificity is equal.

mcqs.question: Which CSS rule defines an animation?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. @keyframes name { ... } defines the steps of an animation.

mcqs.question: How do you make an animation run forever?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. infinite is the keyword for endless iteration.

mcqs.question: In @keyframes, what does from mean?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. from is shorthand for 0% and to is shorthand for 100%.

mcqs.question: What is the correct shorthand order?

options.B: animation: name duration timing-function delay iteration-count direction fill-mode

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The order is: name, duration, timing-function, delay, iteration-count, direction, fill-mode. Only name and duration are required.

mcqs.question: Which timing function is best for a loading spinner?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Spinners should rotate at a constant speed, which is what linear provides.

mcqs.question: What does animation-direction: alternate do?

options.B: Plays forward, then backward, alternating on each iteration

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Alternate plays 0-100% on odd iterations and 100-0% on even iterations, creating smooth back-and-forth motion.

mcqs.question: What does animation-fill-mode: forwards do?

options.B: Keeps the element in its final keyframe state after the animation ends

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without forwards, the element snaps back to its original styles after the animation. With forwards, it stays in the final state.

mcqs.question: Which animation property can JavaScript change to pause an animation?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. animation-play-state: paused freezes the animation. Setting it back to running resumes it.

mcqs.question: How do you apply TWO animations to an element?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Separate multiple animations with commas. Each runs independently.

mcqs.question: Which property can you animate most efficiently?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. transform (and opacity) run on the GPU without triggering layout, making them the most performant.

mcqs.question:
What does this define?
@keyframes fade { from { opacity: 0; } to { opacity: 1; } }
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The opacity goes from 0 (invisible) to 1 (fully visible), creating a fade-in effect.

mcqs.question: Why is animation: spin 1s forwards NOT useful?

options.A: forwards does nothing for non-repeating animations
options.B: spin animations usually loop; forwards has no visible effect on an infinite animation
options.C: You cannot combine forwards with spin

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. forwards preserves the final keyframe state after the animation ends. For a looping animation, there is no 'end', so forwards has no effect.

mcqs.question: What creates a typewriter effect?

options.A: animation-timing-function: linear and width from 0 to full
options.B: animation-timing-function: steps(N, end) with a width animation

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. steps(N, end) advances in discrete jumps, making characters appear one at a time instead of smoothly growing.

mcqs.question: What happens if you apply an animation whose @keyframes name has a typo?

options.B: The browser silently ignores the animation

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Typos in animation names silently fail. Always double-check the @keyframes name matches the animation-name value exactly.

mcqs.question: Which creates a stagger effect where multiple elements animate one after another?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Apply the same animation to multiple elements with different animation-delay values (often using :nth-child) to create a cascade or wave.

mcqs.question: What does prefers-reduced-motion: reduce detect?

options.B: User OS preference for minimized motion (accessibility)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. It detects if the user has asked their OS to reduce motion for accessibility. Use it to disable or simplify animations.

mcqs.question: Can @keyframes contain more than two steps?

options.B: Yes, you can use any number of percentage-based steps

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. You can define as many percentage steps as you want: 0%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%, or any custom percentages.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum valid shorthand for applying an animation?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. At minimum you need the name and duration. Everything else has defaults.

mcqs.question: Which property's value CANNOT be smoothly animated between different values?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. display is discrete with no intermediate values. It cannot be smoothly animated in classic CSS animations.

mcqs.question: Why might animation-delay: 2s be useful?

options.B: To wait 2 seconds before the animation starts (useful for staggering or timing entrance animations)
options.D: To prevent the animation from running at all

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Delay postpones the start of the animation, which is how you stagger multiple animations into a sequence.

codingChallenges.title: Rotating Loading Spinner
codingChallenges.description: Create a circular loading spinner that rotates continuously. Use a border with one side colored to show the spin direction. Duration 1 second, linear, infinite.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use @keyframes and transform: rotate. Must loop infinitely.

codingChallenges.title: Pulsing Button
codingChallenges.description: Create a button that gently pulses (scales from 1 to 1.08 and back) continuously to draw attention. Use alternate to avoid defining both forward and backward steps.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A button that grows and shrinks slowly in a loop.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use @keyframes with only half the animation. Use animation-direction: alternate.
codingChallenges.solution:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
  body { display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; height: 100vh; background: #0f172a; margin: 0; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; }
  @keyframes pulse { from { transform: scale(1); } to { transform: scale(1.08); } }
  .btn { padding: 16px 36px; font-size: 18px; background: #a855f7; color: white; border: none; border-radius: 10px; cursor: pointer; animation: pulse 1s ease-in-out infinite alternate; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
  <button class="btn">Click Me, Aarav!</button>
</body>
</html>

codingChallenges.title: Fade-In on Page Load
codingChallenges.description: Make a heading and paragraph fade in and slide up when the page loads. The heading should animate first, followed by the paragraph 0.3 seconds later. Both should stay visible after the animation ends.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Heading fades in from below, then paragraph fades in.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use @keyframes with opacity and translateY. Use animation-delay. Use fill-mode: forwards.
codingChallenges.solution:
Welcome Ishita

This heading and paragraph fade in from below when the page loads.

codingChallenges.title: Bouncing Ball
codingChallenges.description: Create a ball that bounces up and down continuously. The ball should rise 200px, pause briefly at the top, and fall back. Use cubic-bezier for a natural gravity-like feel.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use @keyframes with 0%, 50%, 100% steps. Use transform: translateY. Use cubic-bezier.

codingChallenges.title: Typing Text Effect
codingChallenges.description: Create a typewriter effect where text appears character by character. Include a blinking cursor. Use steps() timing and ch units for width.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Text types out with a blinking cursor.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use two @keyframes: one for typing (width), one for blink (border). Use steps() timing.
codingChallenges.solution: Hi, I am Priya the dev

codingChallenges.title: Staggered Card Grid Entrance
codingChallenges.description: Create a grid of 5 cards that slide up and fade in one after another when the page loads. Each card should start 0.15s after the previous. Use @keyframes, animation-delay, and :nth-child.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Cards appear in a wave, each one after the previous.
codingChallenges.constraints: Single @keyframes for all cards. Use :nth-child with different animation-delay values. Use fill-mode: forwards.
codingChallenges.solution:
Our Team

Aarav
Priya
Rohan
Ishita
Vivaan
